owing to its diminutive size is rarely bound with the volume to whig
it belongs.”” The gist of it, however, is to be found as an appendix t,
‘“The League of North and South.”’. Another of the author’s owy
works is kept as a curiosity. It is an American edition of * Young
Ireland,’”’ and boldly bears on its title page the words ‘‘ Pirated
Edition *’ |

Shortly before his death Sir Charles gave many of his rarer works
to be ‘‘sold for the promotion of Irish Literature and the circulation

~of National Books among the Irish People,”’ amongst them were two

large 4to volumes, one comtaining about 250 portraits of Irish celebri-

ties from the days of Grattan to those of Butt; the other about 150 !

autograph letters from nearly all the distinguished men who . had
appeared in Ireland for the previous sixty years. Amongst the smaller
books were ‘‘ The Act of Settlement,’’” 1701, called by the Irish ‘* The
Black Act’’; the Strabane edition of the ‘‘ Battle of Augh:rim;” 1785;
‘ Baratariana,’” with the authors identified; ‘‘ The Trial of Hevey V.
Sirr,”’ 1802; ‘‘ The History of Drogheda, with its Ancient Topography,
Antiquities, etc.;”’ by L. C. Johnson, 1826, a very rare volume which
belonged to Davis containing his notes and autograph on title page;

“ The Reform of the Lords,” 1837, the only pamphlet written by

Davis ; four volumes of the State Trials at Dublin and Clonmel :
printed for the Government and not published ; the prompt copy of a
play performed in Richmond prison during the imprisonment of
O’Connell, with many MSS. of Davis, Meagher, Mitchel, and the

other heroes of Young Ireland, as set forth in a now scarce little cata-
logue issued at the time.

AN IRISH AUTHORESS AMONGST THE HEAD-HUNTERS.
1 Although darkest Africa has been travelled from north to south,
¢ and from east to west, and the north and south poles have yielded
{ w their secrets, there still remains somo portions of the ocarth’s sur-
£  face untrodden by civilized man. Amongst these dark places New
Guinea holds pride of place, for the dangers and difficulties that it
| presents. 1In the present century an Irish lady, Miss Beatrice Grim-
shaw, has been the means of throwing a flood of light upon this
little known portion of our Empire. Miss Grimshaw is the third -
f daughter of the late Nicholas Grimshaw, of Cloona, Co. Antrim, Y- -£
where she was born. After an education in Belfast, London and ‘
France, she took to journmalism in Dublin, and for four years edited
“The Social Review ’’ there. But tiring of this, she took to wander-

| g alone in many parts of the world, off the heaten track, and some
seven years ago she went to Papua, New Guinea, commissioned by the
'.'l Australian Government, to assist in the development of the country
by means of publicity in the newspapers and magazines, and the dif-
~ ficulties and damgers of this wild country were of a nature sufficient
to deter all but the most undaunted. Some of the results of her
| travels and observations were set out in * From ITiji to the Cannibal
Islands, 1907 ”’; ‘““In Strange South Seas, 19077 ; ‘“Vait1 of the
; Iﬁ}]a,nds 1907 7’ ; and “The New New Guinea, 1910.”” In addition to

remained until appointed P.P. of Doneraile in. 1895. He was created
D.D. i 1902, and installed Canon of Cloyne in 1903. Despite close at-
tention to his pastoral duties, he found time for the writing of many
fino works , essays, poems and fiction, which gained for him a world-
wide reputation, and have been translated into several European lan-
guages. They are as follows: My New OCurate; Geoffrey Awmstin,
